Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Welcome, everybody. Today I decided just to throw things open and answer the questions
that you guys are thinking about – see if we can’t help you with some pretty specific advice
to get things going.
Let’s get started. You guys were great. The number of questions that came in were just
amazing. The intelligence behind the questions really impressed me. It’s clear that you
guys are serious about what you’re doing. That’s really encouraging to us.
Let’s dive right in.
The first question had to do with an approach to pricing your services for copywriters. It’s
from Cheryl. On another mentor’s blog he recommended offering a menu of services – like
when you go to the gas station and there’s regular, mid-grade, and premium gasoline. If
you go to the Olympics, you’ve got bronze, silver and gold medals.
Cheryl read this blog and liked the idea of creating more or less a bronze, silver, and gold
level of service for her clients so that they can select just the services that they want from
her and not pay for what they don’t need.
Her question is that she’s trying to figure out what should go on the a la carte menu, the
entrée menu, and the full meal. The first thing I thought when I saw that idea was that I
wasn’t crazy about it – for a couple of reasons.
Cheryl, I’d like you just to think about what message a tiered fee structure sends to
prospective clients. If you can structure it in a way so that they’re saying, “Well, I just want
you to write an email blast,” then that is okay.
If it sends the message that if you want good copy, it’s this much, if you want better copy,
it’s that much, and if you want my very best then it’s this much – even subliminally – then
you’re working against yourself.
The other consideration that I have on this is: How does a tiered fee structure facilitate
closing the deal? One of the biggest mistakes that copywriters make when they’re creating
promotions is that they give the prospect too many choices. The prospect then delays
making a decision. As a result, he eventually forgets about the deal or something else
comes up and he doesn’t order it at all.
Any situation where you complicate your client’s decision-making process runs the risk of
having them say, “Wow. I’d better talk to my marketing manager about that,” or “I’d better
talk to my wife,” or “I’d better poll our employees to see how many of these services they
really want.”
To me, this can unnecessarily complicate the entire deal-making process between

-- 1 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
copywriters and clients.
What works for me won’t necessarily work in all markets, but all I can really do here is
share my thoughts on it with you. You can judge whether or not this might apply to your
market.
I just had to go through this with a client and this is how we did it. First we created a rate
card, but we kept it to ourselves. We said that if the client wants copy and Clayton isn’t
writing it and we’re using a copy cub or a freelancer to help us with it, then we’re going to
charge this much per hour.
We did that all the way down through all of the services that we provide. At any rate, here
are the steps that I recommend:
1. 	Decide how much money you want to make. Figure out what your goal is for the
year. What would make you really happy to earn this year?
Figure out how much you need to make this month to hit that target. Double that
amount and divide by 160, which is the number of work hours available to you in
the month. That should yield an hourly rate.
The reason why you want to double it is because you’re not going to be working
100% of those 160 hours writing copy. You’re going to be spending some time
selling. You’re going to be spending some time addressing client crit’s. You’re going
to be taking some time off. A double is a pretty good rule of thumb when coming up
with this hourly rate calculation.
2. 	Identify the kinds of projects you want to work on. If you want to do only
promotional projects, list them: a direct mail piece, an email blast, an online sales
page, a print ad, a television spot – the kinds of projects you want to work on. Just
make the list.
3. 	Calculate the amount of time these projects typically take you. An email blast
takes me eight hours. In Word, my average email blast runs four pages. Divide the
number of pages to arrive at your page rate.
If it takes eight hours, multiply that time by your hourly rate and divide by the
number of pages to arrive at a page rate.
4. 	When asked what you charge for a particular type of job, you need some
information. First, ask them who is going to do the research on this. Do you
have the research all tied up in a nice little bundle? Are they going to deliver the
research, or are you going to have to spend a day, a week, or two weeks doing the

-- 2 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 3 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
research by yourself? That’s a very real part of your cost structure and what you
should pass on to your client in terms of each job.
If the answer is that you have to do the work, calculate how many hours you
think it will take. Add 20%. I like 20% contingencies simply because I tend to be
overly optimistic. I underestimate the amount of time a project will take me and/or
the client will change things along the way in terms of the approach or a sales
argument. Or he’ll say, “Well, I found out that’s not valid,’ or “My lawyer won’t let me
say that,” or whatever.
Things come up. Adding contingency rates really helps. Multiply that times your
hourly rate. Come up with a number that you are going to have to charge for the
research.
Find out how many pages the client expects. I’m a big one on not limiting the length
of a promotion especially, but if the client is expecting a 24-page magalog and you
deliver an eight-page letter, there’s going to be trouble in paradise whether it makes
sense or not. Your 8-pager could outsell the magalog, but if the client’s expectations
are higher and you deliver less, it’s not going to be pretty.
If the client says, “Oh, I don’t know. I see it being a 16-page sales letter,” I would
double the client’s page estimate. Again, that’s your contingency. That’s in case
the page count sometimes increases, the client may require more drafts than your
expect at the outset, and so forth.
5. 	Multiply the page rate by the number of pages, including your contingency. Then
confidently look your client in the eye and say, “For this kind of a job I get $10,000
or $12,000” or whatever the number is.
In many cases, especially with new clients, your client is going to feign shock
and disbelief at your rate – or at least try to negotiate in some way. If you’ve done
everything the way that I’ve laid out for you here, you’ve got lots of wiggle room.
You could offer to do it for less, but at the minimum amount of time that you think
that it might take, and have an allowance in the agreement for adjustments to your
invoice if the page count increases or if more than three drafts are required.
I do that with graphic artists all the time. In fact I just had an invoice like that come
in where we were planning on a 48-page bookalog and it turned out 64 pages. We
had agreed in advance that if the page count changes, he would be paid a certain
page rate for the additional pages.
You could offer to do it for less if the client provides the research. In a lot of cases
they’ll do that because they have researchers on staff or freelance researchers who

-- 3 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
will do that work for less than you’d have to charge.
You could offer to do it for less but ask for a royalty. Royalties typically will run
anywhere from $10 to $50 per thousand in the mail or 10% of the sales, whichever
is more.
On this one you might even consider doing it with no advance and say, “Look,
here’s your choice.” Jim Rutz does this. “You can pay me $500,000 cash now as
a flat fee to write this package, or I’ll write it for free but you have to pay me $50 a
thousand.” A client will almost always go with the $50 a thousand royalty.
Obviously you should only do this if a) you know and trust the client to be honest
with you about how many pieces he mails or how much money the piece is making,
and b) that you have a reasonable expectation of substantial sales or substantial
mailings of the piece.
6. 	I really love this one. It ties into the theme that I’ve been hitting on in The Total
Package for the last two weeks in terms of leveraging freelance relationships with
clients into long-term partnerships.
You could ask the client what other projects he’s got going right now or coming up
soon. Then you could offer him a package deal. Say, “I’ll tell you what. I gave you
my price. It seems to be out of your ballpark. If you’ve got another job for me in the
next two weeks or in the next month, or if you’d like me to take over doing this for
you every month, then I can sharpen my pencil on my regular price.”
Your rationale there is that if I have to go out there and pound the pavement and
recruit another client and go through another learning curve, that costs me money.
Frankly, I’d rather just do another project for you.
One of the things I often tell clients is that the time that I’m worst-suited to write your
promo is when I start writing your promo. The time I’m best-suited to write a promo
for you is after I’ve just delivered one because I know your product inside-out. I’ve
studied your market. I’m good to go. I don’t need to do any market research. I could
dive right in.
There’s good rationale there for offering a discount if the client signs you up for
more than one project.
7. 	Be sure to formalize your agreement in writing. Most clients you work with are going
to be fairly honest with you. I’ve only found a couple of times when a client has tried
to short me on royalties. I caught them at it and things were made right.

-- 4 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The one thing that you can’t recover from is honest misunderstandings or
disagreements over what the terms originally were.
You don’t need a lawyer for this in most cases. A simple letter laying out the
agreement you’ve come to should be plenty. If you have my 6-figure Copywriting
business course, I’ve even included some copywriter contracts in there that you
could replicate if you want something with a little more teeth in it.
Those are my thoughts on that, Cheryl. I hope it helps.
The next question is from Robert:
I’m now working on a couple of courses in reading the classics. I haven’t
selected a niche yet, although I’ve got a couple in mind. How will I know to
begin seeking jobs? Is it just a feeling I’ll have knowing that I’m ready?
Robert, I’m sorry, but I have two daughters and this sounds like the same kind of question
they ask me about getting married eventually.
Here’s my advice to you:
First, do select a niche. It doesn’t have to be something you’re going to be in for the rest of
your life, but it needs to be something that you’re excited about now.
I started out just doing investment promotions. Then I moved onto health. Next I moved
onto politics, and then I moved onto other things.
You need to find an area that you want to focus on. To do that, you should ask yourself,
“What am I passionate about? What subjects am I crazy about? Am I crazy about
economics and investment? Do I love the challenge of writing for the personal finance
market – How to Live Better for Less kind of stuff and Suze Orman kinds of things?
Would I rather write for newsletters or actual supplement products? Would I rather do
catalogues? What is it that I’m really interested in?”
Some of the guys may say, “Heaven for me would be just writing promotions for car
companies or car customizers or car product companies” and for others it would be writing
for companies involved in sports. That’s a huge consideration because whatever niche you
select, you’re going to be spending a lot of time reading and researching in that niche.
It needs to be a subject that you’re interested in. What do you already know a lot about or
would love to learn more about?
See what niches are making other writers the richest. I’m a big fan of the idea that you

-- 5 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 6 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
should die broke and that work is to make money - it’s not a charitable exercise.
When my children come to me and say that they want to be a nurse or they want to be a
missionary to Africa or something else like that, I just tell them, “No. That’s what you do in
your spare time.”
You want to be a musician or you want to write the great American novel – you do that
stuff in your spare time. You get an education and go to work in an area that’s going to
make you the most money the quickest so that you can retire the soonest and have more
life leftover to do the things that you love.
Sometimes just choosing the niche that’s going to make you the most money the quickest
could be the best way to go for you.
Study what’s being done in that niche now. Become your prospect. Buy the products being
offered by the companies in that niche and track what they send to you.
Begin creating promotions for real products in that niche. You get a promotion in the mail
for a particular product and you look at it. You immediately spot four or five things you
could do that are stronger than what’s being done in the promotion you’re looking at. It’s
the same online.
You go to a website and the copy is terrible. It’s awful. It doesn’t even present the benefits
of the product. Sit down, buy the product, and start writing. Write a better web page. Add
to your sample file. Even though they’re self-imposed spec assignments, do it.
Show those assignments to prospective clients. Send it to the company whose web page
you just powered up or the company whose direct mail piece you just strengthened. Then
give them a call and talk to them.
I’ve often talked about how I got my first newsletter promotion assignment when I had $60
in my pocket because I’d just returned a U-Haul truck from moving to Arizona. That was all
the money I had in the world. I was down in Phoenix returning the truck.
There was a publisher there doing research publications. They had a newsletter called the
Daily News Digest. I called up the publisher and said, “I just saw your direct mail piece and
it really stinks. I can beat your response and I’ll do it for free. If I’m successful, then you
have to pay me.” Then I named my price.
He said, “You need to come over here and talk to me.” Just on the spur of the moment
impulse to call the guy, I walked away with an assignment that became pretty much a
career for the next two years. After I increased response on his first newsletter – Daily
News Digest - he immediately retained me every single month to do a promotion for every

-- 6 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
other newsletter he had plus renewal campaigns plus all of the other things that he needed
copy for.
Calling prospective clients with ideas is a great way to go. Don’t chicken out and use
email. Email should only support personal conversation – telephone calls and actual face-
to-face meetings when possible.
Too many people are intimidated or uncomfortable picking up the phone and talking to a
perspective client. They chicken out by using email. It’s never as effective. It doesn’t begin
the bonding process between you and the potential client.
My advice is to shun email. Never do an email when you have a phone close by. Only
use email to respond to perspective clients’ emails, and then only when that makes more
sense than making a phone call.
I want to back up just a second before we move on.
April wants to know:
If you’re entering a new market with no prior experience in it, how should that
affect your price?
April, I’m not out there in the market like you are right now. I’m pretty much booked up with
clients that I’ve had for years, but I never worried about whether or not I had experience in
a particular niche.
What I did was look at what the top writers in the niche were earning, and then I said, “If I
give you a winner, that winner is just as valuable to you as a winner that Gary Bencivenga
gives you or someone else. Therefore, I deserve to get paid the same amount. If I don’t
give you a winner, you won’t have to pay as much because I work on royalties.”
I don’t think you should allow the fact that you’re new to a niche to affect what you charge
for your copy. The key is that copywriting is salesmanship. If you take a good salesman, in
January you can tell him to go out and sell investment-grade diamonds. In February you
can tell him to go out and sell a new brand of toilet plungers, and in March you can tell him
to go out and sell new cars.
He’ll be equally good at all of those things because he understands human nature and he
understands what needs to be said to make the sale and how to identify and overcome
objections and those things.
Enough said. Basically, set your price based on what your financial objectives are and
stick to it. I gave you several fallback positions as I was answering question #1. If a client

-- 7 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
will not work with you on that level, you’re probably better off to just say sayonara and
move on.
Go on to a client who’s more reasonable. A client who won’t work with you on that level is
a client who doesn’t understand and respect the power of good sales copy. That means
that you’re going to have a tough time with that client on every project.
I hope that helps.
Let’s see here. Winnie Anderson says:
I’m working at an ad agency now and going out on my own soon. Should I
wait until my website is done and up to pitch prospects?
Absolutely not. Not having a website up doesn’t mean you don’t have a telephone now or
a way to jump into a car or a city bus and go see a potential prospect.
The website is a nice garnishment. I don’t know if any of you have noticed, but my agency,
Response Ink, doesn’t have a website. We’re working on one. It will be up fairly soon, but
the work comes before all of that.
Networking comes before that. Do whatever you can every day to network, find prospects,
talk to them, share ideas, prove your expertise, let them see your portfolio – both actual
assignments you’ve done and also spec assignments.
Probably the most important thing you can do when you’re talking to a prospect is give
him an idea that he hasn’t had before. Failing that, give him a good idea that he hasn’t had
before or an idea that confirms an idea that he already likes. If you do those things and
make those contacts often enough, you’ll begin building your business in short order.
Hi, John. This is John Guiltor:
Where do I find the phone number of the publisher?
It’s in the masthead of every newspaper and every magazine. It’s usually located in
newsletters at the bottom of Page 2. In magazines it’s somewhere inside the first two or
three pages of the magazine. There’s a little box called the masthead that lists the names
of the major people at that magazine. In the newsletter box it will list the marketing director.
It might use a euphemism like Customer Care Representative or something like that.
Those will be the names. The phone number will typically be there. The good news is the
email address for the Marketing Magazine – in most cases – will not be. You will be forced
to pick up the phone and talk to them which is really good.

-- 8 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Lawton Chiles, the grandson of Governor Walken Lawton of Florida. We just met on our
blog the other day. Lawton wants to know:
Is starting in a small town a huge mistake?
No. It’s not, Lawton. It can be somewhat of an obstacle. When I was getting started, I lived
in L.A. There were a couple of things that I did:
I sent a letter out to a whole bunch of people in the market niche I wanted to write for.
I ended up with a couple of clients on that, but they lived an hour to an hour and a half
away. I lived on the beach and they lived in Santa Monica or Beverly Hills. Even though I
was in a big city, the travel time was fairly extensive.
Wherever you are in Florida, you’re not going to be much farther than an hour and a half
from a good-sized town. I don’t think that will be too much of a restriction for you.
The other thing I did in L.A. was contact all the ad agencies in town and offered them
my services as a copywriter when their copywriters went on vacation or when they were
booked up. I called my little company Copy Overload and I had one other writer working
with me. We ended up working for BBDO and Smith & Hemmings and other really huge
ad agencies in L.A. Being a really huge town, that was helpful.
The bottom line is that with the internet today and cell phones and so forth, I’ve got clients
in Washington DC and L.A. that I’ve never met. These are just people that I’ve contacted
or have contacted me. We wound up finding a way to work together.
I think being in a small town will be more of a mental obstacle to you than it would be
for really finding clients. If you use the mail, email, the phone, and you’re persistent and
helpful to your prospects and your contacts, I think you’re going to do really well.
Question #3 is from Mike.
I’m an ultra-competitive person who hates to lose at anything.
I think I would like Mike.
I’ll easily pull 40-50 hours on a client project trying to get the sales letter
perfect for my client. I’ll pay another copywriter to critique it for me before I
ever turn it over to my client just to make sure the copy is rock solid, yet I still
haven’t produced a big hit for clients with any consistency. This frustrates
me.

-- 9 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 0 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
What am I doing wrong? My ego’s taking a serious beating.
When I first read this, Mike, the first thing I thought was that I would like Mike. The second
thought was that Mike’s going to make it big-time. The third thing that I thought to tell you
was that you’re not doing anything wrong.
The more your ego gets beat up when you lose, the less cavalier you’re going to be when
you take on your next project and the more likely you’re going to be to think it through in
minute detail to avoid making whatever mistakes contributed to the last fiasco.
I’m a big one for talking about my failures. I’ve got to tell you that when I first got started,
I went two or three years without having a single significant winner. I had to go through
client after client after client. Where the expectations were high, I went in and wrote
something that flopped and then dealt with the client’s disappointment.
I know how tough it is, but that toughening process is like how a Samurai sword is made.
They continually pound the metal back on itself. They fold the metal back on itself and
pound it until they get all the impurities out of the blade. The same thing is happening to
you right now.
I know at times it feels like you’ll never get beyond that phase, but trust me you will.
Perseverance is the key.
It also could help you, Mike, to think about the fact that the full weight of the success of
the promotion does not weigh on your shoulders. I just quickly sat down and scribbled out
thirteen different reasons why promotions fail:
1. 	The product could be ill-conceived.
2. 	The pricing could be too low or too high. Sometimes there’s only a certain response
rate you’re going to get, so you’d better ask for the right price – a high enough
price. There are other times when your market for a particular product isn’t going to
pay more than a certain amount. You want to make sure it’s in the right range.
3. 	The guarantee was weak. Clients are famous for chintzing out on the guarantees.
That’s a real credibility killer in a promotion.
4. 	The premium stunk. I’ve had this happen time after time where I took an
assignment from a publisher and he said, “I want you to write this package, but
we’re not going to create any new premiums for you. You have to use premiums
that we’ve already created.” I looked through them and they were terrible. There
was no content or value there. I would have had to lie in order to make them seem
valuable to prospects.

-- 10 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
5. 	The list was poorly chosen. You have no control over this. Especially with smaller
companies, you could go in and the guy just calls up a list broker someplace. To
the list broker this guy is the smallest fish in his pond. He’s not going to make much
money renting this guy’s lists, so he just wants to get him off the phone and get his
check in.
He doesn’t take time to research the available lists out there or other media if he’s
doing paid advertising on the web or radio or TV. The list broker or the person who
made the decision to rent the list could have been out to lunch. You could find out
later on that you were trying to sell prostate relief to a list of women. That’s a huge
one.
When Bob Stone wrote Successful Direct Marketing Methods, he said that the list
selection is 60% of the success of every promotion. You start out wrong there and
you’re screwed from the get-go.
6. 	The promo wasn’t delivered well. We’ve had this situation happen quite often over
my career. We find out that the post office is dumping mail. It tends to happen
especially when there’s discontent among the workers or at peak mail seasons.
You’ll read reports where they find whole bunches of bags of mail dropped down a
sewer someplace.
7. 	Major news events or prospects’ life events depressed readership rates. I had this
happen twice in 2007. In both cases I should have known better because they
weren’t major news events that surprised us. They were holidays that we knew
were coming. We timed our promotion too close to the holiday.
Our peak response period was designed to be over with three days prior to the
holiday beginning, but what happened was that in both cases – Thanksgiving and
Christmas – people didn’t start taking off work or leave town two days before.
Because of the timing of the holidays, they were leaving on the Thursday prior to
Christmas or on the Wednesday prior to Christmas – nearly a week in advance. As
a result, our response was cut short. That happened to me twice last year.
I can give you several other times when major news events have killed response in
the mail. When Reagan was elected and we were selling investment newsletters,
all of the rich, old, white men – the conservative Republicans who subscribed to the
financial newsletters – thought they’d elected the Messiah. They didn’t need advice
anymore because everything was going to go up. That was it. Response just died
for three or four months.
Another time was the O.J. Simpson trials. When those things were on, nobody was

-- 11 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
reading their mail. Another time it was beginning of both Iraqi wars.
Anytime there’s a huge news event, people read their mail less. They read their
email less. They spend more time watching T.V. Response suffers dramatically in
some cases.
I had a client who had taken over a year to build up a big enough war chest to do
a million-piece mailing. He dropped it two weeks before the 1980 election when
Reagan was elected. His response just stopped. It hit a wall.
8. 	The theme for the main headline and/or opening copy failed to resonate. This is a
biggie because a lot of times – especially when you’re working with larger direct
mail companies – they will tell you the theme that they want you to write on.
If it’s an investment promotion, they may say, “We want this to be about inflation,”
or the falling dollar or the crashing stock market or foreign stocks rising or foreign
currencies rising or whatever. “We want a package on that theme.”
In those cases, the choice of the theme is not yours. If you’re up against other
writers on the promotion and you get lucky and you get the theme that people are
thinking about, worrying about, and willing to pay for information on the most, then
there’s a good chance that even weaker copy is going to win. Don’t think for a
minute that those companies don’t know that. They do know that.
They’re going to come back and hire you again if you did a good job with the copy
regardless of whether you won or not.
9. 	The promised benefits were not credible. They had no testimonials. Their main
product claim just wasn’t believable. There were no proof elements to speak of that
they could provide you with.
10. The closing didn’t convince prospects the need to order now. This gets to the offer.
A lot of times you’re too restricted on what kind of offer you can make. Some clients
don’t want to use urgency premiums – early bird bonuses and things like that. If
they do, they give you really weak ones that don’t help snag the sale immediately.
11. Sometimes the order device is difficult to use. That may or may not be laid at your
feet. In most cases that’s the graphic artist who really doesn’t know how to make a
response device simple and accessible. That’s another reason.
12. Sometimes – rarely – there are technical disruptions in order flows. The BRE’s
coming back or the phone lines go down. Banks go down. Servers crash. It doesn’t
happen often, but it’s something worth checking out.

-- 12 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 3 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
13. Maybe you sucked – maybe the copy sucked. The likelihood of that being the case
is extremely remote.
You’ve done your homework. You’ve studied copywriting. You know how to write a
good promotion. You did your best on the project. You thought your way through it.
There’s a 13:1 chance that it was something else or a combination of other things
that really affected response.
Lawton wants to know:
Is Dunhill a good list company?
You know, Lawton, I haven’t been involved in mailing lists for a long time. When I was
involved, the brokerage I worked with was a Dunhill franchise. I came to know the list
pretty well.
Their lists are compiled lists. Somebody went to public records or a phone book and
artificially put those lists together. That’s why they’re so cheap. These are not people
who have qualified themselves by buying something – anything – in the niche area, and
especially not buying anything through direct response.
Avoid compiled lists. When you’re looking at mailing lists or email lists you should be
interested primarily in demographic selects. You shouldn’t care about the age or the sex or
where the people live or any other facts about them. The only fact you should really care
about is: Has this person previously purchased a product like mine at the price that I’m
charging for my product? Have they qualified themselves as being good prospects for my
offer?
Those lists don’t cost $10-$20 per thousand. Those lists of people who have purchased
a product like yours at your price or near your price in the fairly recent past tend to go for
substantially more.
In the financial newsletter field, they can go for $120-$150 per thousand. In the health field
they can go for $100-$130 per thousand. Those are buyers.
A compiled list is basically somebody went to the Motor Vehicle Department and got their
records and compiled a list of everyone with the word ‘doctor’ written before their name.
Then they turn around and sell this list as a list of doctors. You don’t even know if they’re
medical doctors or PhD’s in anthropology or physics. They have a doctor in front of their
name, so they end up on the list. They’re certainly not qualified.
Unless Dunhill has changed their stripes and started offering buyers’ lists, then my answer

-- 13 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
would be that unless you’re doing occupant mailings or something like that, I would find a
better broker.
Cheryl says:
By the way, thanks for the advice on my question. I’ve been dragging my feet
creating that thing. Now I can just whip something up. If they don’t like it, I’ll
find more clients elsewhere.
Good. I love that attitude. Always remember you are the scarce resource. The lack of what
you do is what keeps those companies from growing. It keeps them from expanding their
product lines. You have to have a sense of your self-worth and the importance of the role
that you play.
I want to finish up this thing on flops with another set of thoughts because this is
something all of us deal with. Carline Anglade-Cole has famously revealed that whenever
she has a bomb, she calls me up in tears and screams, “I suck! I suck! I suck!”
Every single time it happens I tell her the same thing:
Grab your wallet. Grab your purse. Jump in that beautiful new Mercedes you drive.
Go to the mall and spend way more money than you can afford to spend. Tomorrow
come back and dive back into the work.
Right now you just need some healing time. You need to deal with your diminished
expectations as a result of this failure. Then you need to come back and figure out
why it failed and learn your lessons.
There are three kinds of flops. This could be of help to you when you have a flop. This
could help you save the day.
1. 	The promotion could have generated a low response rate but high average sale.
What does that mean? It means not many people read the copy – hence the low
response rate – but those who did read it bought like crazy. They were sold.
The culprit in that case was the device used to convince people to read copy in the
first place. It’s the headline and the opening copy. The solution would be to go back
to the client, and you’d be amazed at how few clients understand this stuff, what I’m
telling you now.
Their marketing departments – people who have degrees in marketing – will sit
there with their mouths hanging open, wide-eyed as you try to explain this to them
because they haven’t heard it before.

-- 14 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Low Response means not many people read it. High Average Unit of Sale means
that the copy itself did its job. It did really well. All you have to do is go back to your
client and explain that. Say, “Here are some new headline and opening ideas that I
have. Let’s give these a test.”
The client’s already invested a bunch of money in paying you to write the thing in
the first place. Why not drop another 20,000 or 40,000 or 60,000 pieces in the mail
to two or three panels where you test headlines to see if you can’t get that response
rate up. You do that and you instantly turn this from a loser into a winner.
2. 	The second kind of flop is just the reverse. It’s good response but with low average
sale. The headline and opening copy stopped plenty of people and convinced them
to read the piece, but the sales copy wasn’t compelling enough – didn’t impart
enough confidence to them – for them to want to go with the highest purchase
option – if it’s a newsletter, for example, a two-year subscription rather than a one-
year subscription. If it’s a supplement product, a six-month supply instead of a
three-month supply.
What do you look at then if your average sale was low? They read it. They just
weren’t convinced enough to go with the biggest purchase option.
Here’s your analysis. I just went through it. The culprits could be:
a. 	The product itself: “I’d rather try it before I buy it – I’m not really sure if this is
for me.”
b. 	Price: “I could afford one year, but two years is stretching it. I’d have to talk
to the wife first and she’d laugh at me, so I’m not going to do that.”
c. 	Premiums: You have premium structures that incentivize people to go with
the higher purchase option. Maybe it’s extra bonus reports or maybe it’s
some free supplements. If they go with six months they get the seventh
month free.
Look at the premiums. What can you do to boost the value of the premium
structure for two-year buyers or for the buyers that go for the highest option?
While you’re doing that, also look at your copy on the premiums. Make sure
you did a darn good job of fully dimensionalizing the value of those to your
premiums.
d. 	The offer: that is basically the guarantee – for example, making sure that
two-year people are not punished for trusting you and going for two years.

-- 15 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 6 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
e. 	It could be weak benefit copy. Again, it gets back to: “The product sounds
pretty good. I think I’ll try it, but I’m not fully convinced so I won’t go with the
biggest option.”
f. 	Weak credibility devices: It’s so easy to grab a bunch of testimonials, write
a headline, edit them so they make sense, make them short enough that
they’ll be read, slapping a photograph on them, and making sure that there
are two or three of those on every spread and that every once in a while
there’ll be an entire page or sidebar that’s got a dozen or more testimonials
on it.
One of the cool devices I like to use is I like to co-op the names of
companies that my prospects already trust. If I’m writing a promotion for
an investment product and I want to say, “If you buy options on Natural
Resource ETF’s, Bloomberg says that if you would have bought this option
back on October 16th and spent $5,000 on it, seven days later you would
have had $25,000.”
Bloomberg said that. It’s true. Bloomberg has the research service. You go
to Bloomberg. You look up the options chain. You look at what it did, and
now you can attribute the data to Bloomberg. You can do that in charts and
graphs and tables as well as in the running text.
There’s no charge for that one. It’s just a fun way to bring additional
credibility to your promotion.
The solution is obvious: identify the areas you feel are weak and rewrite the
affected sections. You spend a month on a job. Doing this shouldn’t take you more
than a day or two. It could save the whole project for you. It might even save the
client in some cases.
3. 	The third one is low response and average sale. Analysis: you’re screwed. Culprits:
nothing worked.
At that point, rethink everything. If I get myself into a situation, I just look at the
client and say, “Sorry. I can’t help you with this one. I don’t have a clue.” Just be
honest with them. They know it anyway. By being honest with them, they’ll respect
you and you’ll have a better shot at the next project with them.
Go for it!
You know what – we find that every day on the web. I have a client – I think Chris might

-- 16 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
even be on the call – where we’ve had very low conversion rates on his online promotions.
Chris has done a great job of producing a tremendous flow of leads using pay per click
and banner ads and AdSense and so forth. The people just aren’t converting upfront.
Who knows? Eventually they’ll probably buy something, but we haven’t been making our
money back upfront. The more I looked at his campaign strategy and thought about it, I
thought, “We’re kind of misleading these people.”
We’re telling them to come on over for a free report. When they opt in and give us their
name and email address, they don’t get a free report. They get a promotion piece for
a product. Now not only are they not buying the product, but I’ve got a pissed off new
subscriber to my free ezine. This isn’t for The Total Package. This is for a client’s ezine.
We started thinking about that. You could have written the best copy in the whole world on
that sales page and not sold a blessed thing. In fact, I think we did, but the strategy was
wrong. It sent the message that “You can’t trust what we tell you because we told you that
you’re going to get a free report and instead you’re getting a pitch.”
That’s just one example. There are thousands like it. Tony, thank you for that. You’re
absolutely right.
Gary Ramos says:
What about when a client does not have an established promotion? How do
you determine if your copy is pulling in an acceptable level?
Gary, if he doesn’t have an established promotion, I assume you’re talking about a new
customer acquisition promotion. The client will have his own criteria based on his business
model for whether or not your promotion worked. For example, in direct mail the accepted
model in the newsletter industry is that you want to generate a new customer – and at the
same time generate enough revenue – to offset your mailing costs. In other words, you
want to break even or better.
The amount of money that you collect from that promotion offsets your cost for printing,
list rental, letter shop, and postage to send the promotion out. That’s the criteria most
large newsletter publishers go by. They then pay for the fulfillment of the product by doing
renewal at birth promotions and by selling back-end products – more expensive services
typically. In the health news industry it’s supplements to that new subscriber.
That’s typically how it’s done.
At any rate, he’ll have a number in his head. If he’s paying $560 a thousand to mail the
piece, that means that he’s got to get a response rate and average unit of sale that brings

-- 17 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
him $560 back. That gives you a range of response rates and average sale amounts that
you can hit and still get him that $560.
On a test mailing first time out, that’s his minimal expectations. Hopefully what will happen
is you’ll give him an ROI (return on his investment) of $2,000 which is 400% ROI. On that
test mailing to reduce his risk, he’s mailed the very best list he knows about.
When he starts mailing out and testing other lists, that ROI is going to start coming down
real fast. Your goal is to get as high an ROI as you possibly can on that first one because
that’s going to allow him to load up with more and more lists next time in order to get his
ROI down to 100% or break even. We’re going to look at that a little bit more in a minute.
April wants to know:
Wouldn’t a phone survey of his best current clients give him concrete ideas
rather than guesses to compile emotional reaction to benefits?
Absolutely. If he feels he needs that, I think that’s a great way to go. The fact of the matter
is that he’s been doing this for a while. He’s seen commercial realtors react emotionally to
the various benefits that he provides to them. He’s starting the process with a really good
visceral understanding of what his benefits are – both practical and emotional.
If he still needs more, then sure, take a client out to lunch. Buy him a martini and get him
talking. The process here is basically to understand that every business has a product
that they sell. Sometimes they call it a service but it’s still a product. Every product has
features. It has height, size, weight, color, it does certain things, and it does them a certain
way.
Every product has practical benefits. These are the reasons you give your wife for buying
a product and the excuses that you make to yourself for buying the product. “It will make
me thinner, healthier, it will do this, that, and the other thing.”
Then there are the real reasons why we buy them which are the emotional reasons. For
a commercial realtor, it could be advancement, prestige, freedom from financial worry, the
ego boost of being number one. It could be a ton of things. You’ll find those things as you
talk to them or as you just do business in the space.
James has a challenge for me. He says:
I’ve never seen a good answer to this question in any direct marketing text
or course. If you’re mailing promotions to a fixed audience – like investors –
how often do you mail them, and when/how do you change your promotion?
For example, if you have a universe of 1,000,000 names, once you’ve mailed

-- 18 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
them your control then what do you do?
A new package the next month? Three months later? Or a re-mail of the
original package six months later?
I’ve never seen anyone talk about this in depth.
Well, I’m going to try not to disappoint you, James. Here’s what I came up with.
A total mail cycle is typically six to nine weeks. That gives you the ability to do up to eight
mailings per year. The first two to three weeks are taken – after the mail date –
projecting what your return on investment will be.
The client knows on average by day X he has 50% of his total money back from that
mailing. Typically that day ranges – depending on the promotion and the market – from
two and three weeks (14 and 21 days) from the mail date. It’s a broad rule of thumb, but
it’s usually in there someplace.
At that point in time, historically he’s received 50% of his response. At that point in time of
this mailing he is now ready to say, “These test panels won. These lists worked,” because
he can project his final revenues and therefore ROI. He knows what list worked and he
knows what headline won and he knows which format test won, and all of that.
He then takes a couple to three weeks to prepare his roll-out or ‘continuation’ (the more
proper term) of the promotion. During that period of time he’s doing test panels. He’s
having copywriters and artists create new headline panels and new offer tests and new
price tests and format tests. Maybe this mailing was a magalog and he wanted to see how
a bookalog with the same copy would work.
All of that creative work is happening in that period of time. This is a compressed
schedule, but this is ideal. This is the way it should be. We’ll be talking more about this as
part of the series of articles I’m doing for TTP.
During this period, he is renting the mailing list he’s going to use. They’re being delivered,
and then they go to a merge-purge house. He rents 20 lists. They’re all in the same niche.
There’s going to be overlap. The same names are going to be on several of those lists.
There could be 20-50% duplicate rates between the lists.
Those duplicate names are removed so he doesn’t have to pay postage to have the same
promotion sent to the same guy twice or three or four times. That process has to happen
during this period of time.
He then has two weeks to print, mail, letter shop, address, sort, put postage on, and drop

-- 19 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 0 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
the mailing. Altogether that’s anywhere from six to nine weeks, depending on how efficient
the mailer is.
Some mailers will get more aggressive. Let’s say for example your order processing can’t
handle a 20-million piece mailing once every six to eight weeks. Or let’s say you want
more frequent mailing data than you get just mailing one mailing after another sequentially.
Then you create what’s called a bicycle.
You have several mailings all happening overlapping. Mailing 1 is here. You’re projecting
your ROI and moving onto preparing your roll-out, then printing and mailing. While you’re
doing that, you’re this far along and now you’ve got your second group of mailing lists.
They’re producing their own ROI’s and suggesting their own tests and so forth. You’re
moving ahead with those when the time is right.
Your third group of lists comes in. You’re able to project ROI and you move that one, and
so on. You can load up this bicycle as much as you want to and get your mailings really
huge if you want to. I’ve gone as high as three wheels at a time when we were being really
aggressive and good mail-in times.
I’m getting to the answer. I just wanted to take you through that process.
Here’s what typically happens. You have a test mailing. The first answer is: You want to
mail your promotion to your best names every six to eight weeks. That’s the ideal.
Here’s what typically happens. Here’s the ROI break-even line of 100%. It will cost you
$600 a thousand to mail. You’ll get $600 back for every 1,000 pieces mailed. That is this
response level here.
You go out with your test mailing, Mailing #1. It comes in way the heck up here. What
does that look like – 220% of cost? You’ve got a really great ROI. Everyone’s all excited
we’ve got something we can mail. Step two is the continuation. In this mailing here, you’ve
mailed maybe 50-100 lists, but you’ve only mailed a small representative sample of each
list – probably 20,000 names is my favorite.
It’s my magic number because it produces statistically reliable results in most cases.
There’s a whole calculation thing that you can do there that has to do with the size of the
total universe to come up with the number that you need to mail to come away with a
reliable number. 20,000 per list is a good rule of thumb.
Mailing #1 goes out and you’re only mailing representative samples of each list. In those
samples are selected on what’s called an Nth Name Select. The lists are selected in
geographic order by zip code. If there are 100,000 names and you want 20,000 names,
you tell the computer to pick every 5th name. You’ve got a good geographic sampling that’s

-- 20 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
spread evenly across the country. You mail those list fragments.
Mailing #2 comes along and you just did 220%. The first thing you’re going to want to do is
a continuation. You’re going to want to mail more of the names on the list that you tested
that were successful, that did well for you.
In this mailing, some lists pulled 600%. Some lists didn’t even break even. In here now
you’re going to want to do continuations on all of the lists that did break even or better for
you. In some cases if the lists are huge, you’re not going to want to mail the entire list the
very next outing. You may want to be a little bit more cautious than that.
Different mailers have different approaches. If I get a good test here, I just go ‘balls to
the wall’ here and do a full roll-out. A lot of people will test 20,000 names here of 100,000
name list. Maybe they’ll test another 50,000 here and then they’ll finish up on another
mailing.
At the same time you do this mailing there is another one that isn’t shown here that has
a bunch of test lists in it. You’ve proven your promotion here, so now you’ve got to prove
mailing lists. There’s another mailing that happens simultaneously. Here you have a whole
bunch of lists that have prospects on them that have very similar characteristics to the lists
that worked, but you haven’t tested them yet so you’re going to go cautiously.
That happens here, too. At some point you’re going to get to the point where you’ve
mailed all of the names of all of these lists that have worked well with this promotion piece.
At some point you’re going to get to where you’re starting to repeat yourself and you’re
starting to go back. The same people are going to start seeing the same promotion piece.
That’s when attrition starts happening.
Obviously it could start happening anywhere along in here for reasons we’ve talked about
before – major events in the news, mail delivery problems, or maybe the list broker just
gave you the best names off the list and lied to you about it. It happens sometimes. Of
course, those brokers don’t stay in business long.
Now it’s time to re-mail to the same prospects. Bang! You get yourself a nice little attrition
rate, but you’re still above break-even. This is a red flag that it’s time to get serious here
and start thinking about what you can do to protect your ROI.
The next re-mail you’re going to get even lower. That package attrition is predictable. You
can pretty much look at history and be able to see it happen, and even predict. On a re-
mail my average list attritions 20%. If I got $1,000 per 1,000 return the first time I mailed it,
I’m only going to get $800 per 1,000 the next time I mail the same package to the next file.
What I like to do is do our test mailing. That proves that the package works and it tells us

-- 21 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
which lists among our favorite lists are going to work well for us. Then we have Mailing
#2 which is a continuation. Immediately with that mailing I want to test 2-3 new headline
panels and I may want to test some other things, too.
I may want to test price. I may want to test offer. When I say ‘offer’ I mean instead of just a
one and two-year offer on a newsletter, I may want to add a third year option. I may want
to add a continuity panel, “Send no money now. We’ll bill you $30 a month for the rest of
your life until you tell us to stop.”
We put our tests in there. On the very next mailing, that gives us some time now because
this takes longer. I want to test alternative formats. If this is a magalog that we tested here,
the first thing I’m going to want to say is, “How will that copy work in other formats?”
I may do a six by nine package with a 16-page sales letter. I may turn the magalog copy
into a bookalog, which is a six by nine thing that looks like a book. It’s got a four-color
cover like a book and the insides are two colors.
I may want to test a tabloid. I may want to test a slim jim. My options are unlimited there.
Of course, what happens is you kind of get into a pattern here because over time you
notice the safest piece to mail on the front-end is a magalog. If the magalog works, then a
bookalog is going to work better. I found that out in the health industry. I’m going to default
to a bookalog test here. I might add another panel as well.
After this mailing is over with, what do I know? I’ve got a new headline and opening copy
that’s kicking butt. It’s kicking the original control all over the parking lot. I’ve also got a new
format that gives me another lift.
Now I’m going to combine the two. I’m going to go out in a promotion here with a new
control. It does get more complicated than this, or it can. I may, for example, want to test
the original piece with my new headline and then test the alternate format – the bookalog
or whatever – with my original headline and with a slightly different one that feels more
bookalog-ish (one that feels more like a book).
There are a lot of ways that you can play that, but your goal with that second mailing,
using the same copy just with a different headline and different format, is to get your ROI
back up here. You repeat this process over and over and over again until the copy that you
wrote originally just, no matter what you do to it, you’re not able to get back up to your ROI
targets.
When you don’t get to your ROI targets what happens? What that means is that the
individual lists that you’re mailing are – each one of them – performing less well. At some
point, some of them will stop hitting break-even altogether. When that happens, you can’t
mail them because your objective is to break even.

-- 22 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 3 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
What does that do? It shrinks the size of your mailing. The mailings start getting smaller.
When you go a couple of mailings and you are unable to get a lift and you see your ROI
falling to the point where they’re in danger of dipping below the break-even level, it’s time
to get serious about writing a whole new fresh package.
That’s essentially the process that we go through. The answer to the basic question is:
You want to hit every qualified prospect you can on every list that’s doing break-even or
better as often as possible. In the meantime, keep the promotion piece as fresh and as
current as possible with whatever headline, copy, price, offer, test you can do and also by
changing of formats.
A lot of times what will happen is that you’ll be doing a magalog here and you’ll test a
bookalog here, and they both work equally well. That’s wonderful because now you can
mail a magalog here and then mail a bookalog here and mail a magalog here and again
that’s another way of slowing the attrition and ROI that would otherwise happen using the
same copy over and over.
I hope that helps.
Nathan is working with a client who’s launching a new financial trading service in the U.K.
He wants to know:
Would I put together a complete selling message covering every possible
benefit and plucking every possible emotional banjo string?
I play a Stratocaster, Nathan.
Or would you narrow the focus on a couple of key things for the first launch
message and then create an extended launch period?
This gives you 40-page sales page or longer. This is basically doing it piecemeal. Another
way to do a product launch – should I go for the jugular or give the prospect time to let his
guard down first by a softer graduated introduction?
The first thing, Nathan, is that some of this depends on who you’re going to. If you’re going
to your own customers – people who already know you – your messaging tone is going to
naturally tend to be a bit lower-key anyway because you already have a relationship with
them. You’re friends. They trust you. They like your products. You like them because they
pay you for them.
You write differently to friends than you do to strangers. There may be some
considerations there. Let me show you two different models that work.

-- 23 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The first is a progressive promotion. You create a long copy sales page and you post it
on the web. It has educational components, why you need to be worried about inflation
or why you need to be excited about gold prices going up, why you need to be in oil, or
whatever. It presents every benefit of the product, all the credibility elements.
This thing could be 40, 50, or 60 pages long. It really doesn’t matter. You’re not going to be
sending it out time after time after time to your prospects or customers.
Then you go to your file and you do topical emails if this is for customers. You say, “Look at
this! Gold jumped $40 yesterday. It’s really time to get on board. Let me show you the best
way I know to make money on gold. Click here.” Bang – they go to the long copy sales
page.
You can do the same thing with direct mail and blog postings. I love doing live online
events. We did one yesterday and we sold $1 million worth of financial services between
noon and midnight yesterday off of a live online event that drove people to a long copy
sales page.
You can then follow by sending videos of that live event and a transcript of the live event to
people – each one with a link on the page driving people to the long copy sales page. You
can offer a discount tied to watching that event and have a deadline for accepting it. Do a
countdown to the end of the discount.
You can also do a countdown to the first trade that the service is going to issue. Basically,
this is kind of a progressive thing. You can have a lot of fun with this.
This event is an hour long because it’s content, content, content, with the last ten minutes
being a pleasant chat with attendees about what the product does. This does your hard
selling for you. That’s progressive promotion.
A more linear promotion might go this way: You have an invitation to the live event, a
countdown to the live event, the live event, and recording transcripts. This has to happen
in a particular order, whereas the other one you can do pretty much in any order. Again, all
are driving to the long copy sales page.
I gave you some date things here. I know they look better on the PDF you got than they do
here.
The answer is: If you use long copy on all of your communications online to clients, they
are going to tune out and turn off after the first couple of contacts. That’s okay because
online is free. It doesn’t cost you anything to send an email.

-- 24 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Offline where you’re paying $500-$700 per thousand to mail a direct mail piece, you can’t
afford to do a series. You can’t afford to stretch it out. For those kinds of promotions you
want long copy and you want to present every reason why the prospect should buy, every
single credibility element you can come up with, and present a persuasive case.
Understand that offline when you’re doing direct mail, direct mail is not linear. Some
people read magalogs front to back. Some read them back to front. Some just let them
flop open in the middle and start reading wherever their eye falls. People who are more
methodical might start at the beginning, but might scan through the pages just checking
out the pictures and the headlines and sub-heads, only reading body copy when
something interests them. It gives the prospect more freedom to jump around.
It’s much harder to do that on a long landing page online. In the mail, I’ll probably go to my
grave defending long copy. On the web, you have the luxury of multiple contacts for very
little or no cost. The best way to leverage that is to change it up constantly.
Communicate through webinars, teleseminars, videos, MP3’s, podcasts, email blasts, and
landing pages. Change it up and take advantage of the medium.
This question #7 is about handling drop-offs when the product or service is delivered
over a one-year period. They’re selling a $995 product and they want to do a continuity
program essentially. You pay a twelfth of that every month for twelve months.
Phillip says:
Where the potential drop-offs is certain points, do you have any suggestions
on how to maximize retention?
Phillip, here are a few ways to do it.
1. 	Offer price and premium incentives to those who pay cash up front. Move as much
of your people into the cash up front situation while also making the payment plan
available to those who can’t or are reluctant to pay the full amount upfront. That
gives you the best of both worlds.
You snag the most cash possible up front if you have good incentives for people
who pay cash up front, and you don’t have to worry about people dropping out of
the continuity program because you’ve got their money.
The only thing that can happen then is if they exercise the guarantee at some point,
you’re going to have to pay a refund. Fewer people will do that than will cancel a
continuity program.

-- 25 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 6 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
2. 	You can offer customers the opportunity to convert to annual once per quarter.
This is called renewal at birth in the print newsletter industry. I signed up for
your continuity program. I’m going along fine. A quarter has passed now. A full
membership now costs less than it would have had I bought it before because I’ve
already made three payments on it essentially. You can structure an offer for people
to convert them at that point.
3. 	You can over-deliver with unadvertised surprise bonuses which makes them want
to hang around to see what you’re going to give them next month. You can tease
each month’s benefits in advance. “Be sure to be with us next month because
here’s what we’re going to do together.”
4. 	You can offer a big bonus at the end of each year. A guy I was just talking to has
a coaching program. At the end of twelve months if you’re with him for the twelve
months and you graduate from the program, you’re invited to a live conference that
other people are going to have to pay $2,500 to attend.
If you’re only three months away from earning that perk and you really want to
attend it and it’s going to save you $2,500, you’re much more likely to stick with the
program.
The answer to your main question is eight. For some reason, the eighth month on a
continuity program tends to be the month in which most people see their biggest drop-
offs. Also, there are other clients that I’ve had who tell me that their overall average on
continuity programs is right around eight months.
It will vary from industry to industry, but that’s the point in time when the people have been
there and done it. They’ve seen your product. They may have moved on. They may be
less interested in whatever it is that you’re delivering. They may have had negative results
if it’s a financial product.
Eight seems to be the point in time when you really need to focus on retention.
Next question is from Chris:
Any tips for increasing conversion on main squeeze pages?
These are my thoughts. I want to preface this by saying that there’s not a day goes by that
I find that I don’t learn something new about the web and find that some philosophy I’ve
had and clung to is no longer valid.
Here are the rules that we go by now. We try to keep them fairly short. We don’t want
them to be reading assignments. If the copy has to be longer, that’s fine. Keep it lively and

-- 26 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
absolutely don’t go for length for length’s sake.
People are going to click a link. They’re going to go to this landing page expecting a free
report. The landing page is an interruption in their life because they were expecting to click
that link and have their free report. Then they have to give their name.
Keep them short. Confirm what they’ll get. Try to make the entire process take as little
time as possible. Establish your credibility somewhere on the page. Let them know who
you are, what you’ve done, what others have said about you – one or two key testimonials
might be enough.
Demonstrate your privacy policy. Demonstrate the fact that their email will never be
shared with anybody. ‘Demonstrate’ means to have a policy that links to a policy window
or something else that really elevates this to a solemn vow. If possible use a third party to
guarantee somehow that names are protected.
Demonstrate your cancellation policy. I like to see a bug on that page that says something
like, ‘Safe unsubscribe’ or something like that so that I know there’s someone else looking
out for me. If you turn out to be a spammer, all I have to do is click a link that’s at the
bottom of every email and you disappear from my life.
Show your business credentials. We’re going through this long thing right now to get on
Better Business Bureau preferred vendor status. It takes a lot of work – including in our
case changing the Damas Oliver Corporation because we’re Damas out in Florida, but we
operate out of North Carolina. It’s worth it, though.
You’re a total stranger to people who see you online. Showing that you’re a Better
Business Bureau trusted vendor is going to go a long way towards making providing their
email address easier to do.
Do what you say you’ll do. If the page says, “Just give us your name and email address.
We’re going to email a report to you immediately,” do that. Don’t forward them to a page
that is a report. You’re saying that you need their email address because you’re going to
send their report by email or the link to the report by email, so do that.
Add a viral element. When they add their email address and they click ‘send’ or ‘enter’
have a script that pops up and says, “Do you have friends or family that are also interested
in this subject? If so, enter their names here. We won’t record them. Edit the email
message below any way you like and click ‘send’. Be a hero to your friends and family.”
Consider adding a force-free trial of some kind. When people opt in on these pages, they
are giving you their email address ostensibly to get a free report. They’re also giving you
permission to contact them. That has to be stated on the page.

-- 27 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
We usually do that by offering them a free ezine. You can also have a little pre-checked
checkbox that says, “Please also give me three months of Safe Money Report free. If I like
what I see, I’ll do nothing. After my three-month trial you’ll bill me for x dollars per month”
or however you want to set up the deal. It’s worth a test.
Having said all of this, I just want to point out that recently we tested a squeeze page
that begins the report discussion on the squeeze page. It starts delivering some value on
the squeeze page and then says, “To get the full report, please give us your first name
and email address.” My understanding is, and I haven’t really looked into it in detail, it is
working quite well.
We’re going to be looking at that and playing with that idea as well. Those are some ideas
on squeeze pages.
Robert from Bonn wants to know:
Do we recommend any German copywriters? Are there any?
Robert, I don’t know any. I wish I could help there. You might want to call Norman Rentrop
up in Bonn and ask to talk to somebody in marketing there. They’re great guys. Norman is
a prince. They use copywriters quite extensively. They’ll probably be able to give you some
leads.
Next question is from Harun:
What position tactics would you recommend for copywriters to take right
now?
I think it’s less about positioning yourself as it is about selecting clients. If you read – and
I know you did – last week’s issue or this Monday’s issue of The Total Package, times
are getting tough. It’s companies that have products that can be perceived as being
indispensable or helpful at a time like this that are going to do quite well. Those are the
companies that you want to be associated with, especially if you’re on royalties.
Keep in mind the environment we’re moving into. Right now, for example, a coin company
or a gold company is going to do quite well in this environment because all this money the
Fed’s are pumping in is going to trigger higher inflation. We’re already starting to see it.
That’s why gold is selling at nearly $900 an ounce. The old all-time high was in 1980 and it
was $875 interday.
Gold has taken off like a rocket. It’s almost tripled in price in the last few years just
because of people’s concerns about inflation. Demand is up. People want coins and ingots

-- 28 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page  of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
as a safe refuge. It’s a great business to be in at a time like this.
On our blog there was a guy – and I forget his name – who has a car that will run all week
or all month on $38 of gas. It’s very fuel efficient and not very expensive. Anything that
saves people money right now, anything that helps them ensure their job security, anything
that helps people when they lose their jobs are areas to think about.
Lawton says:
Many of my clients are not willing to spend money on bigger ads in the paper
or to try to have ads look more like editorials (advertorial ads). How can I get
clients that have money to advertise in other markets to test and try direct
mail?
It’s not always an easy thing to do because people get so set in their ways. Clients are
guilty of saying the dumbest thing that you’ll ever hear as a copywriter or marketing
consultant. They say, “We’re doing fine now. Why change things?”
The answer is: You can always do better. It’s hard sometimes to get people to really
understand that.
Jonathan has a German copywriter for us.
Marion…
Your guess is as good as mine on the last name. I’m sorry, I’m not good at German last
names, especially when they’re as long as my arm. The website is www.copywriting.de and
the telephone is 49-022472035. The name again is Marion Schinelpfennig. If you didn’t get
it that time around, you can get it from the recording.
Kevin also has one:
Liane Pirscher. Her email is liane@pirscher.de.
So there are two copywriters. You ask, you get. Thanks, guys. Fantastic.
Lawton, my suggestion would be to go out and find a competitor or a near competitor
who’s making money hand over fist with these other media. Go in and set your client
down. Get as much intelligence as you can on them in terms of how many customers
they’re bringing in.
Look at the SRDS. Look at the hotline names. It will tell you how many names they’re
bringing in every 60 days. It it’s substantially more than your client is bringing in with his

-- 29 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 30 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
approach, you may be able to get some traction with that.
Basically it’s a sales job. You just have to reason with the client and provide enough proof
to convince the client that it’s worth a test.
Lawton also wants to know:
What’s the most effective way to track advert’s in the paper and online? I see
a little code, but how does that help?
Very quickly, it’s called a key code. This is how it’s used in direct mail. This key code here
tells you what mailing list was being mailed. In this code are all kinds of little goodies. Part
of the code relates to the name of the list. Part of the code relates to any special selects on
the list – like we may have just selected ‘men’ or ‘women’ or a particular zip code.
The marketing director who mailed this can take one look at this and tell you exactly –
based on this alpha-numeric code – the list and the list segment and list selects that were
used.
Here we have the promotion key. This alpha-numeric code tells you about this promotion
here. If I call in, the operator is going to say, “Would you please give us your ID? It’s on the
back cover of the booklet you received.” He’ll just read this number. Obviously the order
form is on the reverse form of this back cover so that if they mail the order form, we also
capture this information.
The promotion key here – this code – can tell us whether it was a bookalog, magalog, a
letter, or whatever. It can tell us what headline was on it. It can tell us what the offer was. It
can tell us what the premiums were. These codes can be crafted any way that you want to
capture any information you need.
That’s what those things are for. If it’s something in a newspaper, the code will be much
shorter than this because there aren’t going to be all the permutations. They’re not going
to be testing a bunch of headlines, offers, prices, and they’re certainly not going to be
doing list segmentation like you do in direct mail. The code could be as simple as a four-
digit code that tells you that the ad ran in Barron’s in January.
Basically, when somebody orders, what happens is this information is entered on their
customer record. For time immemorial we can go back. We know where that guy came
from and we know what he responded to. We can track how well buyers from that source
perform long-term compared to buyers from other sources and so forth.
This is a crash course in key codes.

-- 30 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 3 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
These are from Mark:
In the past, corporate retrenching has lead to discharged employees to
pursue new career paths with less dependency on corporate jobs.
He then asks four questions:
1. 	Do you see this happening again now?
The answer is yes. The last time around – I forget the number – it was something like
20 million new home-based businesses started after 9/11. It was some huge, ridiculous
number. I definitely see it happening again.
It’s partially from people who have been fired and are looking for a way to replace that
income. It’s partially from people who are afraid of being fired and want to have a fallback
position. It’s partially from people who are just worried about inflation and want to add
income. They’ll do it part-time.
Yes, I see that as being a huge opportunity in this recession
2. 	Do you think these newly minted entrepreneurs are a good market for info
products?
Absolutely. If there’s anything they need, it’s a road map to getting to where they want to
go. Yes.
3. 	What markets, categories, or other areas do you believe will yield the
greatest returns? Where’s the hungriest crowd?
I think they’re all hungry. I think one of the things that you need to look at are the kinds of
people that are likely to be laid off. If you’re looking at blue collar people, then I think online
marketing is a huge topic. How to Get Rich on eBay or How to Run a Successful eBay
Business or online auction business – anything along those lines would be good.
Remember, though, that the people who are getting laid off are not independently wealthy.
The largest number of people who are going to lose their jobs in this recession – or who
are concerned about losing their jobs in this recession – make a good, decent living but
they’re not rich. They’re not going to be able to invest $100,000 or $200,000 or $500,000
to start a business. They’re going to want something that they can do fairly quickly with
simple tools in their own home and build it from there.
That’s definitely a consideration. Personal finances are always good. In a recession health
is okay. We still all have bodies and hearts, and we’re all still fat and bald. We all want to

-- 31 of 36 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 6 • Makepeace Secrets Exposed! Q&A Session 1
Transcript 	Page 3 of 36
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
make improvements.
Jonathan just said:
The best market now is realtors.
Parris got started during the real estate recession. I think it was in the 80’s. He was in
real estate and he wrote a book How to Sell a House in a Down Market. He sold a whole
bunch of them, but he didn’t understand direct marketing at the time. He had no follow-up
product. Even though his promotions were successful, he lost money. Keep that in mind.
